Advent of Code is a yearly coding challenge that runs every December, where a new programming puzzle is released each day leading up to Christmas. Each problem is a mix of logic, problem-solving, and creativity, and it’s become a popular way for developers to practice and sharpen their skills.

I originally started doing it just to learn a bit of Python. Nothing serious—just curiosity and a way to get more comfortable with coding through small daily problems.

But over time, it slowly turned into a tradition.

Now, every year when Advent of Code comes around, I try to complete it again—but this time in JavaScript. It’s less about finishing everything perfectly and more about the process: showing up each day, solving problems, and seeing how I’ve improved since the last time.

What started as a learning exercise has become something I genuinely look forward to every December.

---

CSSBattle is a daily(ish) coding challenge where you try to recreate visual targets using the smallest and most efficient amount of CSS possible. It’s all about precision, creativity, and sometimes completely abusing CSS in ways it was probably never intended for.

I’m a frontend developer, so naturally CSS is something I work with all the time—and honestly, I kind of love it.

Even if it has *that reputation*.

You know the one: you move one thing slightly to the left, and suddenly everything breaks in a completely unrelated way. Layouts shift, spacing disappears, and somehow a `div` from three components ago is now ruining your life.

![CSS moment](https://i.gifer.com/1oUQ.gif)

It’s frustrating for most people… but weirdly fun for me.

CSSBattle takes that feeling and turns it into a game. Instead of fighting CSS, you’re *playing* with it—trying to understand it better, push it further, and sometimes just accept that yes, absolute positioning might fix everything (or destroy it completely).

It’s one of those things I genuinely enjoy coming back to, because it constantly reminds me how powerful (and chaotic) CSS can be.

---

### References


[CSSBattle](https://cssbattle.dev)

[Github Repo](https://github.com/bgarrido7/CSSBattle)

Have you ever been asked to recommend a movie and suddenly forgotten every single one you’ve ever seen?

Yeah… that happens to me more often than I’d like to admit.

But even when I *do* remember, everything I like is just… scattered. Some things are on one app, others on a different website. Movies in one place, books in another, games somewhere else, music somewhere else again. It never really felt like *my* collection—just pieces of it spread all over the internet.

So I made this.

It’s a simple place where I can store everything I love—books, video games, music, anime—all in one spot. No switching between platforms, no trying to remember where I saved something. Just one app that actually reflects my taste as a whole.

It’s not trying to replace those big, specific platforms. It just connects everything in a way that makes sense for me. I can keep track of what I’ve experienced, organize it however I want, and always have my favorites ready—whether I’m recommending something or just revisiting things I love.

And on a personal note, this project was also my excuse to finally try Vue.js for the first time. It gave me a way to build something meaningful while exploring a new framework and learning as I went.

Basically, it’s my personal media space.

And now you can see it too.

## References

[Github Repo](https://github.com/bgarrido7/my-likes)

Watching the Oscars has become a yearly ritual for me.

Once the nominees are announced, I go through everything carefully and try to watch as many of the nominated films as I can before the ceremony. Then I print out the official Oscars ballot, sit down, and start filling in my predictions category by category.

There’s something about having it on paper that makes it more real—like I’m officially locking in my choices for the year.

I’ve built up a small collection of these papers over time. Each one captures that specific year and who I thought would win. Looking back on them has become part of the experience.

But what really keeps me coming back is how it makes the Oscars night itself more exciting. Having my own predictions sitting next to me during the ceremony turns every announcement into something personal. Every win is either a hit or a miss for me, and it makes watching feel a lot more engaging than just following along.

So I built this app.

It’s a way to immortalize my predictions and the Oscar wins year after year, so that even if one day I inevitably lose those paper ballots, the history of what I chose and what actually won is still preserved in one place.

---

[The Academy Awards](https://www.oscars.org/oscars/ceremonies/2026)

[Github Repo](https://github.com/bgarrido7/oscars)

I’ve been playing Pokémon since I was a teen. And when I first got into it, it honestly felt like rock-paper-scissors… but on steroids. You start simple—Fire, Water, Grass—but then it just keeps expanding. Electric, Ground, Psychic, Dark, Fairy… suddenly there are 18 different types, all interacting with each other in ways that actually matter a lot in battle. At some point, I remember finding this small, simple game. Nothing fancy—just a way to pick two types and see which one would win. No story, no battles—just pure type matchups. But what really made it stick was the gamification of it. It didn’t feel like studying or memorizing anything. I was just playing, testing myself, getting things right (and wrong), and trying again. And somehow, without even realizing it, I ended up memorizing every single type advantage. It turned something that could’ve been overwhelming into something intuitive and fun. Eventually, I tried to find that game again… …and I couldn’t. So I decided to recreate it. This app is my version of that simple idea: a clean, focused way to explore Pokémon type matchups. No unnecessary complexity—just pick types, test interactions, and learn naturally as you go. Whether you’re just starting out or trying to master the full type chart (like the one from Pokémon Database), it’s all about making that learning process feel effortless and engaging. And on a personal note, this project was also my first real dive into AngularJS. It started as a small idea, but it became a way for me to experiment, learn, and build something from scratch while recreating a piece of nostalgia I couldn’t find anymore. It’s not trying to replace the games. It’s just bringing back that one small experience that made everything finally click. --- ### References ["Pokémon types & type chart" | pokemondb](https://pokemondb.net/type) [Github Repo](https://github.com/bgarrido7/PokemonAdvantages)

I’ve been playing Pokémon since I was a teen.

And when I first got into it, it honestly felt like rock-paper-scissors… but on steroids. You start simple—Fire, Water, Grass—but then it just keeps expanding. Electric, Ground, Psychic, Dark, Fairy… suddenly there are 18 different types, all interacting with each other in ways that actually matter a lot in battle.

At some point, I remember finding this small, simple game. Nothing fancy—just a way to pick two types and see which one would win. No story, no battles—just pure type matchups.

But what really made it stick was the gamification of it.

It didn’t feel like studying or memorizing anything. I was just playing, testing myself, getting things right (and wrong), and trying again. And somehow, without even realizing it, I ended up memorizing every single type advantage. It turned something that could’ve been overwhelming into something intuitive and fun.

Eventually, I tried to find that game again…

…and I couldn’t.

So I decided to recreate it.

This app is my version of that simple idea: a clean, focused way to explore Pokémon type matchups. No unnecessary complexity—just pick types, test interactions, and learn naturally as you go. Whether you’re just starting out or trying to master the full type chart (like the one from Pokémon Database), it’s all about making that learning process feel effortless and engaging.

And on a personal note, this project was also my first real dive into AngularJS. It started as a small idea, but it became a way for me to experiment, learn, and build something from scratch while recreating a piece of nostalgia I couldn’t find anymore.

It’s not trying to replace the games.

It’s just bringing back that one small experience that made everything finally click.

“Pokémon types & type chart” | pokemondb
